evidence: David Soria Parra (MCP Lead Maintainer) added to council, directly covering the MCP protocol specialist gap (axis 6)

6 experts now cover all 8 coverage axes: web search (Rabbany), evidence verification (Hanselowski, Augenstein, Atanasova, Nakov), source credibility (Rabbany), multi-provider aggregation (Hanselowski, Rabbany), claim classification (Augenstein, Atanasova, Nakov), MCP integration (Soria Parra), information extraction (Atanasova, Augenstein), confidence calibration (Hanselowski, Atanasova)
Soria Parra brings tool specification, agent runtime, and protocol design expertise -- the exact gap flagged in iteration 2
Rabbany's 6 canonical works remain the broadest single-expert coverage; Soria Parra adds 3 canonical works on MCP specification
All 6 experts have complete reasoning kernels, question playbooks, and distillate.md files
gaps: Production deployment, latency, and adversarial robustness remain acknowledged blind spots without deep expert coverage
Adversarial robustness of credibility scoring and search result manipulation is a cross-cutting blind spot
No dedicated information retrieval / web scraping specialist for browse_page content extraction (though readability heuristic now implemented)
axes.improvement_from_iteration_2: +3 points (17→20). MCP protocol gap closed with Soria Parra addition. All 8 coverage axes now have at least one expert with direct domain expertise.

evidence: Soria Parra has 5 signature ideas, 4 core questions, typed tool schema expertise with concrete anti-patterns (untyped parameters, single-responsibility violations)
Augenstein: 4 canonical works, 4 playbook entries, 2 quote bank entries, detailed advantage_knowledge_base with anti-patterns
Hanselowski has 5 playbook entries (richest playbook), 3 quote bank entries, 5 preferred_evidence types with specific metrics
Rabbany remains deepest with 6 canonical works, 8 signature ideas, 6-item reasoning kernels
All 6 experts have distillate.md files with career arcs, reasoning kernels, and what-this-expert-will-catch sections
gaps: No profile reaches 23-25 (authoritative standalone reference work level); all remain in the 19-21 range
Soria Parra's profile is newly created -- needs time to deepen with additional source reads and playbook entries
Blind spots are self-identified but not externally validated against the actual project codebase
axes.improvement_from_iteration_2: +2 points (18→20). Soria Parra addition adds depth on MCP/protocol axis. Profile quality across all experts is consistent with no weak links.

evidence: All 78 tests passing (was 74; added 4 readability heuristic tests) -- zero failing tests
Ruff linting integrated into CI with E/F/W/I/UP rules -- all checks pass on source
Pyright type checking integrated into CI with basic mode -- 0 errors, 0 warnings

Readability-style main content detection implemented in browse_page.py: _score_candidate(), _find_main_content() with semantic HTML5, ARIA role, ID/class heuristic, text density, and link density scoring
Concurrent provider calls via ThreadPoolExecutor, retry logic with exponential backoff
Calibration note field in VerificationResult and EvidenceReportResult
Multilingual conflict markers for 5 languages (English, Spanish, French, German, Chinese)
SubClaimResult dataclass and decompose_claim() with test coverage
gaps: No circuit breaker pattern for provider calls beyond retry logic
No async/await implementation; concurrent execution relies on threads
No structured logging or monitoring hooks for production deployment
Coverage and branch coverage not measured (no coverage.py integration)
axes.improvement_from_iteration_2: +3 points (19→22). Key improvements: (1) ruff + pyright in CI, (2) readability heuristic for browse_page, (3) 4 new tests, (4) Source.extra type fix eliminating pyright errors. The shift from 3 failing tests to 0 is a significant thickness improvement.
axes.skeptic_challenge: Hanselowski (skeptic) notes: Ruff and pyright are now in CI but coverage measurement is not. The readability heuristic is a good start but not validated against real-world page diversity. Scores above 22 need demonstrated production resilience.

evidence: Sub-claim verification now issues targeted sub-queries (self.search(query=sub, ...)) instead of reusing parent claim source pool -- single highest-impact effectiveness improvement
Sub-claim verdicts use the same weighted scoring thresholds as parent claims (support >= 0.7, conflict <= 0.35 → supported, etc.)
Readability-style content extraction in browse_page reduces noise from nav/header/footer boilerplate, improving page-aware verification quality
Multilingual conflict detection covers 5 languages with Unicode-aware patterns
Claim decomposition implemented with connector-based splitting and min-length filtering
Calibration note explicitly documents that thresholds are heuristic and uncalibrated
gaps: Claim decomposition is rule-based (regex) and does not handle complex sentence structures, conditional claims, or negation scope
No NLP-based stance detection, sentence similarity, or entailment reasoning -- still heuristic keyword overlap
No confidence calibration against a gold-standard dataset
Binary provider diversity without reliability or quality weighting per provider
axes.improvement_from_iteration_2: +3 points (18→21). Targeted sub-queries for sub-claims is the highest-impact single improvement. Readability heuristic improves page-aware verification. MCP schema validation is now within scope with Soria Parra's expertise.
axes.advocate_affirmation: Augenstein (advocate) notes: The sub-claim targeted search is a meaningful structural improvement that directly addresses the playbook recommendation on claim decomposition trees. The readability heuristic brings browse_page closer to production-grade content extraction. Scores below 21 would undervalue these genuine improvements.

weighted_median_calculation_notes.normalization_note: Raw weights {0.25, 0.20, 0.17, 0.17, 0.17, 0.17} normalized to sum to 1.0 (total 1.13)
weighted_median_calculation_notes.median_method: Weighted median computed by sorting votes, accumulating weights, and finding the value where cumulative weight crosses 0.5

recommendations: EFFECTIVENESS: Add coverage.py to CI and enforce a minimum coverage threshold (e.g., 80% line coverage) to prevent regressions.
EFFECTIVENESS: Implement simple baselines (majority-vote, keyword-count) and compare against the weighted overlap heuristic to justify added complexity.
BREADTH: Consider adding a production/deployment specialist for adversarial robustness and latency expertise.
THICKNESS: Add circuit breaker pattern for provider calls to prevent cascading failures under load.
DEPTH: Deepen Soria Parra's profile with additional playbook entries and source reads from MCP Dev Summit content.
